deduplication.strategy: Conservative - only remove rationally identical rules
deduplication.rationale: Analyzed all rules for rational duplicates. Removed only those expressing identical business logic while preserving rules with meaningful differences in thresholds, contexts, or conditions.
deduplication.analysis_notes:
Process summary and conservative decisions: 
- I prioritized conservatism: I only merged rules when scope, numeric timeframes or thresholds, patient population, care setting, provider type, and regulatory context aligned. Where any key parameter differed (e.g., 24 hours vs 48 hours; 'prior to anesthesia' vs 'prior to procedure'; Medicare-specific vs general claims; attending-physician-only vs facility-wide), I either created a separate group or excluded that rule from the merge. 
- Primary selection used the stated priority: completeness then specificity, then source clarity. Where one rule included additional useful detail (e.g., validated tool, documentation requirement) I selected it as primary and preserved the additional details in the merged_description. 
- Scoring followed the provided rubric: semantic equivalence (major weight), numeric precision (timeframes and thresholds matched exactly in merged groups), contextual alignment (same setting/population), source consistency (same implied regulatory/authority), and consequence alignment (same enforcement/penalty language). 
- Confidence is 'high' for groups with identical timeframes, identical scope, and identical consequences; 'medium' where minor phrasing differences implied small variations in expectation (e.g., general annual updates vs specific notification requirements). 
- Remaining rules not listed as duplicates were judged to have meaningful variations (different timeframes, different population/payer, different provider types, or unique requirements) and should be retained individually in the knowledge graph. 
If you want, I can (a) produce a second-pass that attempts to merge some of the lower-confidence pairs (e.g., immediate vs within 24 hours) with explicit notes about tightened/weakened obligations, or (b) produce an updated rule set (primary + merged metadata) ready to ingest into the knowledge graph with provenance links to original rule IDs.

dependency_analysis.rationale: Identified prerequisite, sequential, conditional, complementary, contradictory, and override relationships between rules to enable proper execution ordering and conflict resolution.

examples: A surgical report is entered into the medical record within 24 hours of the operation.
An anesthesiologist updates the patient's record immediately after surgery.
The appendectomy performed on John Doe is documented in his medical record.
The knee replacement for Jane Smith is recorded in her medical record.

dependencies.rationale: These documentation rules are overlapping and together provide redundancy and support for enforcement and auditing of post-op documentation timelines.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Using both rules in audits reduces chance of missing non-compliant instances and provides multiple citations for remediation.

business_rules.rule_name: Surgical Procedures Must Be Documented in Medical Records Immediately
business_rules.description: All surgical procedures must be documented in the patient's medical record immediately after completion.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all surgical procedures performed in any healthcare setting.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to document may result in legal consequences and regulatory penalties.

source_reference.source_text: Surgical procedures must be documented in the medical record immediately.

examples: The surgical report for the appendectomy was completed and added to the medical record immediately after surgery.
The cholecystectomy documentation was filed within 30 minutes of the procedure's completion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Legal liability for failure to document

examples: The appendectomy performed on John Doe is documented in his medical record.
The knee replacement for Jane Smith is recorded in her medical record.

dependencies.rationale: Complementary documentation rules ensure consistent expectations regardless of care setting and reduce gaps in the medical record.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Aligned documentation rules reduce regulatory exposure and support coding/billing activities.

business_rules.rule_name: Surgical Procedures Must Be Documented in Medical Records Within 24 Hours (Variant 2)
business_rules.description: All surgical procedures must be documented in the patient's medical record no later than 24 hours after the procedure.
business_rules.conditions: This rule applies to all surgical procedures performed.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to document may lead to incomplete medical records and potential legal issues.

source_reference.source_text: All surgical procedures must be documented in the patient's medical record...

examples: A surgeon completes the operative report within 24 hours after surgery.
An anesthesiologist documents anesthesia details in the medical record.
The appendectomy performed on John Doe is documented in his medical record.
The knee replacement for Jane Smith is recorded in her medical record.

dependencies.rationale: Complementary documentation obligations ensure that no matter the setting, documentation timing and completeness expectations are applied consistently.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Consistency across documentation rules reduces the risk of incomplete records and supports billing and quality reporting.

business_rules.rule_name: Claims Must Be Submitted by Hospitals Within 30 Days of Service Date
business_rules.description: Acute care hospitals must submit claims for services rendered to health plans within 30 days of the service date.
business_rules.conditions: This rule applies to all claims submitted by acute care hospitals.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to submit claims within 30 days may result in claim denial.

source_reference.source_text: Claims must be submitted by hospitals within 30 days of the service date...

examples: Claim for services rendered on January 1, 2023, must be submitted by January 31, 2023.
Hospital fails to submit claim within 30 days, resulting in denial.
The hospital submits multiple claims for surgeries performed in a month.
Claims are submitted to the health plan for reimbursement.

dependencies.rationale: BR_DOCUMENTS_RELATIONSHIP_001_001 requires surgical procedures be documented within 24 hours. Claim submission rules rely on that documentation to support billing; without the documentation, claims are likely to be denied or down-coded.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If documentation is incomplete or missing at time of claim submission, claims may be denied, delayed, or audited.

dependencies.rationale: BR_AUTHORIZES_RELATIONSHIP_003_001 requires plan authorization before performance of surgical procedures. If authorization is required for coverage, then claim submission and expected reimbursement depend on having had prior authorization.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Submitting claims for procedures performed without required authorization is likely to result in denials, appeals, and potential patient financial responsibility.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_011_001 selected: explicitly requires informed consent documented in the medical record prior to any surgical procedure and is concise and comprehensive.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All grouped rules require written informed consent prior to surgical procedures with the same scope (surgical procedures) and the same consequence (legal/regulatory penalties). Only differences are wording or slight emphasis (e.g., stating documentation explicitly). No differing timeframes, populations, settings, or regulatory bodies.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Written informed consent must be obtained from the patient prior to any surgical procedure and documented in the patient's medical record. Consent must include sufficient information about risks, benefits, and alternatives as required by applicable law and accreditation standards.
merged_examples: Example: A patient scheduled for an elective cholecystectomy signs a written consent form and the signed form is filed in the medical record prior to the operation.
Example: For outpatient arthroscopy, a clinician obtains and documents written consent explaining procedure risks, alternatives, and possible complications before the procedure.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: CMS Conditions of Participation; Common law informed consent principles; The Joint Commission standard on informed consent.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_PATIENT_CONSENT_012_001 selected: clearly specifies the 24-hour minimum timeframe which makes it the most precise and actionable.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require written informed consent at least 24 hours prior to scheduled surgical procedures. Timeframe and scope are identical; only phrasing varies.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A written informed consent must be obtained and documented at least 24 hours before the scheduled surgical procedure (for procedures that require anesthesia), unless governed by different facility policy or law (e.g., emergency exceptions).
merged_examples: Example: Elective hysterectomy consent completed and documented one day before planned surgery.
Example: Pre-op clinic documents signed consent at least 24 hours prior to anesthesia start time.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: The Joint Commission informed consent standards; CMS guidance on elective surgery consent.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_006_001 selected: explicitly ties consent to the anesthesia step and documents prior-to-anesthesia requirement.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require written informed consent to be obtained and documented prior to the administration of anesthesia for surgical procedures. The regulatory intent and timing (before anesthesia) are identical; only wording differs.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Written informed consent must be obtained and documented before administering anesthesia for any surgical procedure. Documentation must be available in the medical record at the time of anesthesia induction.
merged_examples: Example: An anesthesiologist confirms a documented consent for the planned operation and anesthesia before induction.
Example: Pre-op nurse verifies presence of signed consent form in chart before transporting patient to OR.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: Anesthesia informed consent expectations (facility policies, The Joint Commission), CMS conditions relevant to surgical services.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_019_001 selected: explicit phrasing 'before administering any treatment or medication' and enumerated identifiers makes it the most comprehensive source rule for clinical settings.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All grouped rules require verification of patient identity using at least two unique identifiers prior to treatment/procedure. Scope (all clinical settings) and consequence (patient harm/legal risk) are consistent. Differences are limited to wording.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Prior to any treatment or procedure, the patient’s identity must be verified using at least two unique identifiers (for example: full name and date of birth). Verification must be documented per facility policy.
merged_examples: Example: Before medication administration, nurse confirms patient name and DOB against wristband and chart.
Example: Before blood transfusion, staff verifies two identifiers and documents verification on transfusion record.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: The Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goals; CMS Conditions of Participation for patient identification.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_005_001 selected: explicit statement 'prior to any medication administration' and example identifiers make it the clearest medication-specific rule.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require two identifiers before medication administration. They match semantically and in context (medication safety), with identical implied consequences. Wording differs only.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Before administering any medication, healthcare providers must verify the patient's identity using two unique identifiers (e.g., name and date of birth) and document verification per policy.
merged_examples: Example: RN verifies two identifiers at bedside before giving an insulin dose and documents verification in MAR.
Example: Pharmacist cross-checks name and DOB before dispensing unit-dose medications for administration.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: The Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal on patient identification; Medication administration policies.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_020_002 selected: specifies validated tool and 8-hour timeframe (most actionable).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require a fall risk assessment within 8 hours of admission for inpatient admissions. One rule mentions 'validated tool' explicitly — merged rule preserves that enhancement.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A fall risk assessment must be completed within 8 hours of a patient's admission to inpatient care, using a validated risk assessment tool where available. Assessment findings and interventions must be documented in the medical record.
merged_examples: Example: Within 4 hours of admission, nursing completes the Morse Fall Scale and documents risk and interventions.
Example: For an elderly admission, fall precautions (bed alarm, non-skid socks) are initiated based on validated assessment within 8 hours.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: The Joint Commission patient safety goals; facility fall prevention policy.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_DOCUMENTATION_003_001 selected: clear, general statement requiring medical record update within 24 hours across care settings.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Grouped rules require updating patient medical records within 24 hours of treatment/encounter. Scope is broad (all settings) and consequences align. Minor wording differences only.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Medical records must be updated within 24 hours following any patient encounter, treatment, or procedure to reflect care provided, clinical findings, and orders. Documentation should be complete, legible, and attributable.
merged_examples: Example: Clinic visit notes entered into the EHR within 24 hours of patient encounter.
Example: Post-procedure nursing notes filed within 24 hours after outpatient procedure.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: CMS Conditions of Participation; The Joint Commission documentation standards.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_SURGICAL_PROCEDURE_DOCUMENTATION_001_001 selected: explicit requirement for surgical procedure documentation within 24 hours, broad coverage of inpatient and outpatient settings.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All grouped rules require documentation of surgical procedures in the medical record within 24 hours post-procedure (or equivalent wording). Timeframes and scope are the same; differences are phrasing and additional detail (e.g., include anesthesia type, complications) which have been preserved in the merged description.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: All surgical procedures must be documented in the patient's medical record within 24 hours of completion. Documentation should include procedure details, anesthesia type, findings, and any complications or pertinent post-operative instructions.
merged_examples: Example: Operative note completed and filed in chart within 12 hours after surgery, including anesthesia and complications.
Example: Outpatient procedure report entered within 24 hours describing procedure and immediate outcome.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: CMS surgical services CoPs; The Joint Commission perioperative documentation standards.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_MEDICAL_RECORD_RETENTION_001_001 selected: clear statement of 10-year retention minimum and broad applicability.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules specify retaining medical records for a minimum of 10 years from last service. Scope and numeric threshold are identical; only wording differences exist.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: All medical records must be retained for a minimum of 10 years from the date of the last service provided. Retention applies to inpatient and outpatient records and must comply with applicable state and federal requirements.
merged_examples: Example: A patient's chart for an outpatient surgical visit must be retained for at least 10 years after the visit date.
Example: Discharge summaries are archived and retained for a minimum of 10 years from last service.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: Applicable state record retention statutes; CMS record retention guidance.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_ATTENDING_PHYSICIAN_CREDENTIALING_001_001 selected: most explicit about two-year reappointment cycle for attending physicians and effects on privileges.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All grouped rules require credentialing/recredentialing every two years for attending physicians to maintain privileges. Numeric timeframe (two years / 24 months) and scope match. Differences are primarily in phrasing.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: All attending physicians must complete credentialing/reappointment every two years (24 months) to maintain hospital clinical privileges. Credentialing includes verification of licensure, privileging, and ongoing competency per facility policy and regulatory requirements.
merged_examples: Example: Annual file review plus reappointment packet completed at month 24 to renew privileges.
Example: Provider privileges suspended if recredentialing not completed by the 24-month due date.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: CMS Conditions of Participation for staff privileges; hospital credentialing bylaws.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDENTIALING_002_001 selected: clearly states annual license verification and applies to all physicians providing care.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Grouped rules require annual verification of medical licenses for physicians. All match on numeric timeframe (annual), target population (all physicians), and consequence (unable to practice/suspension).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: All attending physicians must have their medical licenses verified at least annually to ensure they remain in good standing with state medical boards. Verification must be documented in credentialing files.
merged_examples: Example: Credentialing office confirms active state medical license each calendar year and files verification.
Example: Provider flagged for follow-up if license verification is not current.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: State medical board verification standards; CMS CoP expectations for staff qualifications.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_005_001 selected: clear statement of 12-month claim submission window applying broadly to health plan claims.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules require claim submission to health plans within 12 months of date of service. Scope and numeric precision are identical for general (non-payer-specific) claims, only wording differs.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: All claims for services rendered must be submitted to the applicable health plan/insurer within 12 months of the date of service to preserve eligibility for reimbursement, subject to payer-specific rules.
merged_examples: Example: An outpatient surgery claim submitted 9 months after DOS is eligible for processing.
Example: Claims older than 12 months generally face denial by most payers unless an exception applies.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: Common commercial payer timely-filing rules; provider billing compliance policies.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_INSURANCE_CLAIM_SUBMISSION_007_001 selected: explicitly Medicare-focused with the 12-month deadline, making it authoritative for Medicare claims.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Grouped rules are Medicare-specific requirements to submit claims within 12 months. Same payer (Medicare) and identical numeric deadline; only phrasing differs.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: All Medicare claims must be submitted within 12 months from the date of service to be eligible for reimbursement in accordance with Medicare timely filing requirements.
merged_examples: Example: A provider must submit a Medicare Part B claim within 12 months of DOS to avoid denial.
Example: Claims submitted after 12 months require appeal with documented exception and payer approval.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: CMS claims timely filing policy; Medicare Claims Processing Manual.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_HEALTH_PLAN_AUTHORIZATION_001_001 selected: broadly phrased and applicable to all surgical procedures requiring plan authorization; clear on consequence (non-payment).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All grouped rules require prior authorization from the health plan before performing surgical procedures when coverage requires it. Scope (surgical procedures requiring coverage), timing (prior to procedure), and consequence (denial/non-payment) align. Differences are in phrasing and whether rule is labeled 'consent' or 'authorization'.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Prior authorization from the patient’s health plan must be obtained before performing surgical procedures that require insurance authorization. Lack of prior authorization may lead to denial of claims and financial liability for the provider or patient.
merged_examples: Example: Elective spinal fusion requires plan prior-authorization before scheduling the procedure.
Example: Pre-certification for outpatient arthroscopy received and documented prior to surgery date.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: Typical payer prior authorization policies; facility authorization procedures; CMS guidance where applicable.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_HEALTH_PLAN_COVERAGE_DETAILS_012_001 selected: explicitly requires providing coverage information to potential members prior to enrollment.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules require that health plans provide clear and comprehensive coverage information to potential enrollees prior to enrollment. Scope and timing align; differences are wording and emphasis.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Health plans must provide clear and comprehensive coverage details, including benefits and exclusions, to potential enrollees prior to enrollment to support informed decision-making.
merged_examples: Example: Enrollment packet includes summary of benefits and exclusions before member completes enrollment.
Example: Online plan comparison tool accessible during enrollment with coverage details.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: ERISA/marketplace disclosure requirements where applicable; state insurance disclosure rules.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_HEALTH_PLAN_COVERAGE_014_001 selected: clear annual requirement and general applicability.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Grouped rules require health plans to provide coverage details to members at least annually. While intent and population align, some entries specify slightly different frequencies or phrasing; merged rule captures annual/update requirement. Because format/frequency language varies, confidence is slightly reduced.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Health plans must provide clear and comprehensive coverage details and updates to members at least annually, and notify members of material changes during open enrollment or as required by law.
merged_examples: Example: Annual member handbook update mailed or posted online with summary of coverage changes.
Example: Notice to members when benefits change mid-year affecting cost-sharing.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: State and federal disclosure rules; CMS guidance for regulated plans.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CMS_REGIONAL_OFFICE_COMPLIANCE_001_001 selected: concise statement requiring CMS regional offices to perform annual compliance reviews across facilities; broadest and clearest representative.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All grouped rules require annual compliance reviews/inspections/audits by CMS regional offices of healthcare facilities participating in Medicare/Medicaid. Scope (CMS jurisdiction), frequency (annual), and consequences align. Differences are phrasing only.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: CMS regional offices must conduct compliance reviews/audits of healthcare facilities under their jurisdiction at least annually to ensure adherence to CMS regulations. Facilities participating in Medicare/Medicaid must be available for these surveys.
merged_examples: Example: Annual CMS survey of an acute care hospital to evaluate Conditions of Participation compliance.
Example: Regional office schedules and conducts audits of provider compliance with federal program requirements.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: CMS survey and certification guidance; Program Integrity manuals.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_JOINT_COMMISSION_STANDARD_COMPLIANCE_001_001 selected: direct statement requiring annual demonstration of compliance with The Joint Commission standards.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Rules grouped share the same requirement: healthcare organizations must demonstrate compliance with The Joint Commission standards annually. Scope, timing, and consequences (loss of accreditation) are consistent. Variations are in wording or whether documentation/submission is specified.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Accredited healthcare organizations must assess and demonstrate compliance with applicable The Joint Commission standards at least annually to maintain accreditation and ensure patient safety and quality.
merged_examples: Example: Annual internal self-assessment against Joint Commission standards and documented corrective actions.
Example: Submission of annual compliance findings to the governing body as part of accreditation readiness.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: The Joint Commission accreditation requirements and standards.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_TREATS_DOCUMENTATION_007_001 selected: clear 24-hour documentation requirement for attending physicians and applicable to all physician-patient encounters.
duplicate_groups.rationale: The grouped rules require attending physicians to document treatments provided in the medical record, frequently with a 24-hour timeframe. Scope (attending physicians), timing (immediate/24 hours), and consequence (compliance findings) align. Some entries used 'immediately' or 48 hours; those with 48-hour stricter/looser timeframes were excluded where inconsistent. Merged rule preserves 24-hour expectation as the common operational standard.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Attending physicians must document treatments and encounters in the patient's medical record, generally within 24 hours of the encounter (or immediately when required by local policy). Documentation should include date/time, nature of treatment, plan, and signatures/identification.
merged_examples: Example: Physician documents progress note and treatment plan in EHR within 24 hours of bedside visit.
Example: Post-procedure physician operative or procedure note entered within 24 hours describing interventions and plans.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: CMS documentation requirements; The Joint Commission documentation standards; hospital medical staff bylaws.

deduplication_details.analysis_notes:
Process summary and conservative decisions: 
- I prioritized conservatism: I only merged rules when scope, numeric timeframes or thresholds, patient population, care setting, provider type, and regulatory context aligned. Where any key parameter differed (e.g., 24 hours vs 48 hours; 'prior to anesthesia' vs 'prior to procedure'; Medicare-specific vs general claims; attending-physician-only vs facility-wide), I either created a separate group or excluded that rule from the merge. 
- Primary selection used the stated priority: completeness then specificity, then source clarity. Where one rule included additional useful detail (e.g., validated tool, documentation requirement) I selected it as primary and preserved the additional details in the merged_description. 
- Scoring followed the provided rubric: semantic equivalence (major weight), numeric precision (timeframes and thresholds matched exactly in merged groups), contextual alignment (same setting/population), source consistency (same implied regulatory/authority), and consequence alignment (same enforcement/penalty language). 
- Confidence is 'high' for groups with identical timeframes, identical scope, and identical consequences; 'medium' where minor phrasing differences implied small variations in expectation (e.g., general annual updates vs specific notification requirements). 
- Remaining rules not listed as duplicates were judged to have meaningful variations (different timeframes, different population/payer, different provider types, or unique requirements) and should be retained individually in the knowledge graph. 
If you want, I can (a) produce a second-pass that attempts to merge some of the lower-confidence pairs (e.g., immediate vs within 24 hours) with explicit notes about tightened/weakened obligations, or (b) produce an updated rule set (primary + merged metadata) ready to ingest into the knowledge graph with provenance links to original rule IDs.

dependencies.strength_justification: Written informed consent before any medical treatment/surgical procedure is a direct legal prerequisite to administering anesthesia or performing surgery.
dependencies.rationale: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_001_001 establishes the high-level legal requirement that written informed consent must be documented before medical treatment or surgical procedures. BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_006_001 specifically requires written consent prior to administering anesthesia for surgical procedures. The general consent rule must be satisfied before the anesthesia-specific rule can be executed.
dependencies.impact: If the general consent requirement is not satisfied, anesthesia cannot be lawfully administered; this exposes the provider and facility to legal action, CMS penalties, and possible suspension of services.
examples: A patient is scheduled for surgery but no documented written consent exists — anesthesia must not be administered.
Consent form unsigned at time of pre-op — surgical/anesthesia team must halt the process until a valid consent is obtained.

dependencies.strength_justification: A rule requiring consent at least 24 hours before surgery is a strict temporal prerequisite for any anesthesia-related activity tied to that surgical event.
dependencies.rationale: BR_PATIENT_CONSENT_012_001 mandates consent be obtained and documented at least 24 hours prior to scheduled surgery. BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_006_001 requires written consent prior to administering anesthesia. Therefore the 24-hour consent rule must be satisfied before anesthesia administration for scheduled surgeries.
dependencies.impact: If the 24-hour prior consent is missing, performing anesthesia for scheduled surgery would violate policy and risk cancellation, legal liability, and regulatory sanctions.
examples: Elective knee replacement is scheduled; consent signed on day of surgery (less than 24 hours)—surgery/anesthesia may be canceled per policy.
Pre-op clinic documents consent 26 hours prior — anesthesia may proceed per the 24-hour rule.

dependencies.strength_justification: Obtaining a thorough history/H&P within 24 hours of admission is a common clinical prerequisite to a valid informed consent (moderate dependency / best practice).
dependencies.rationale: BR_PATIENT_DOCUMENTATION_022_001 requires the patient's medical history to be documented no later than 24 hours after admission. Informed consent (BR_PATIENT_CONSENT_012_001) requires that the patient understands risks/benefits — which depends on a documented history and assessment. Completing the H&P supports an informed consent process.
dependencies.impact: If history/H&P is not documented in a timely manner, the consent process may be incomplete or legally vulnerable, potentially delaying procedures or exposing the facility to audit findings.
examples: Surgeon attempts to obtain informed consent before a documented H&P — the consent may be challenged as not fully informed.
H&P documented within 12 hours supports a robust informed consent discussion the next day.

dependencies.strength_justification: Patient identification using two unique identifiers is a legal/clinical prerequisite to safe medication administration and is critical to preventing harm.
dependencies.rationale: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_019_001 mandates verification of patient identity using at least two identifiers before treatment or medication. BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_005_001 requires verification prior to medication administration. The general identification requirement must be satisfied before any medication administration action can proceed.
dependencies.impact: If identity verification is not completed, medication administration must be withheld; failure to verify can lead to wrong-patient medication errors, harm, and regulatory sanctions.
examples: Nurse prepares medication but cannot confirm two identifiers — medication administration is paused until identification is verified.
Two-identifier check performed and documented prior to giving IV antibiotic — complies with both rules.

dependencies.strength_justification: Annual license verification is a necessary component of multi-year reappointment/credentialing; failure to verify blocks reappointment (strong dependency).
dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDENTIALING_002_001 requires annual verification of medical licenses. BR_ATTENDING_PHYSICIAN_CREDENTIALING_001_001 requires physicians to undergo a reappointment/credentialing process every two years to maintain privileges. Annual license verification is an essential step feeding into the reappointment process.
dependencies.impact: If annual license verifications are not performed, reappointment cannot be completed reliably and physician privileges may be suspended, disrupting clinical services.
examples: License verification flagged expired license during annual check — reappointment is held until resolved.
All licenses verified annually, enabling smooth two-year reappointment.

dependencies.strength_justification: Proper credentialing of physicians complements quality reporting because accurate privileges and documentation underpin valid quality metrics (strong complementary relationship).
dependencies.rationale: BR_ATTENDING_PHYSICIAN_CREDENTIALING_001_001 ensures physicians have current privileges to perform procedures. BR_QUALITY_MEASURE_009_004 requires hospitals report surgical quality metrics. Accurate credentialing complements quality reporting by ensuring metrics are attributed to appropriately privileged providers and that data reflect legitimate clinical activity.
dependencies.impact: If physicians are not properly credentialed, quality reports may be inaccurate or challenged, potentially leading to penalties or reputational harm.
examples: Surgeries performed by an uncredentialed physician may invalidate reported complication/readmission rates for that provider.
Robust credentialing ensures quality reports accurately reflect facility performance.

dependencies.strength_justification: Different required completion timeframes for medical record elements (48 hours vs. 24 hours) create a moderate conflict about acceptable timelines.
dependencies.rationale: BR_PATIENT_DOCUMENTATION_008_001 requires the medical record to be completed within 48 hours of admission; BR_PATIENT_DOCUMENTATION_022_001 requires the patient's medical history to be documented no later than 24 hours after admission. These overlapping but differing timeframes create a contradiction when the scope of 'medical record completion' vs 'history documentation' is not clearly delineated.
dependencies.impact: Conflicting timeframes increase audit risk, cause inconsistent staff workflows, and may lead to compliance findings if auditors expect the stricter 24-hour standard but documentation follows a 48-hour policy.
examples: Facility policy uses BR_PATIENT_DOCUMENTATION_008_001 (48h) as the standard, but a surveyor cites BR_PATIENT_DOCUMENTATION_022_001 (24h) — potential deficiency.
Clinicians unclear which elements must be done at 24h vs 48h leading to delayed histories.

dependencies.strength_justification: Different specified timeframes for documenting informed consent (>=24 hours prior vs 'within 30 days prior') create a moderate conflict in temporal requirements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_PATIENT_CONSENT_012_001 mandates consent at least 24 hours prior to scheduled surgery. BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_020_001 requires consent documented in the medical record within 30 days prior to surgery. The 24-hour minimum requirement and an allowance for consent up to 30 days prior may be compatible but can be interpreted inconsistently (e.g., is same-day consent acceptable if documented within 30 days but not 24 hours prior?). This ambiguity leads to contradictory operational rules.
dependencies.impact: Inconsistent application of consent timing could lead to canceled procedures, legal exposure, or survey findings depending on which rule auditors apply.
examples: Consent signed 3 days prior — acceptable under 30-day rule and meets 24-hour minimum; no conflict.
Consent signed on the day of surgery — allowed under 'within 30 days' but violates the 'at least 24 hours prior' rule for elective procedures, causing conflict.

dependencies.strength_justification: Documenting identity verification steps serves to validate that the two-identifier check was performed; moderate dependency for audit/QA purposes.
dependencies.rationale: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_005_001 requires verification of identity prior to medication. BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_019_001 is the general rule defining two identifier usage. Documentation of the verification acts as validation evidence that the broader two-identifier policy was executed.
dependencies.impact: If identity checks are not validated in records, audits may treat the checks as not performed even if done, increasing compliance risk.
examples: MAR entry includes two identifiers and staff initials — validates adherence to the two-identifier policy.
No documentation of identity check — audit flags noncompliance with two-identifier standard.

dependencies.strength_justification: Complete and timely surgical documentation is a necessary verification for claims; payers and internal billing need complete procedure documentation to support submissions.
dependencies.rationale: Billing compliance (timely claim submission and substantiation) depends on accurate procedure documentation (timely and complete surgical report). Claims are validated against the medical record during audits.
dependencies.impact: Incomplete or late surgical documentation can result in claim denials, delays, and audit exposure.
examples: Surgical report not present at time of claim submission → payer requests record or denies claim.
Post-op complications not documented within timeframe → coding and reimbursement disputes arise.

dependencies.strength_justification: Retention requirements presuppose the existence of the medical record; records must first be created/updated before they can be retained for the retention period.
dependencies.rationale: You cannot retain what does not exist — surgical procedure documentation is an input into the long-term medical record retention requirement (minimum retention period).
dependencies.impact: If records are not created or are incomplete, retention obligations cannot be satisfied and legal/regulatory exposure increases.
examples: Surgical report not filed → provider cannot demonstrate compliance with 10-year retention for that episode.
Incomplete medical record entry → retention store contains incomplete data and audit requests reveal gaps.

dependencies.strength_justification: Timely updating of medical records (within 24 hours) supports accurate claims; billing validation uses chart updates to support services billed.
dependencies.rationale: Claims accuracy and defensibility depend on contemporaneous documentation. The billing compliance rule assumes that records are updated in a timely manner to reflect services billed.
dependencies.impact: Delayed updates may impair coding accuracy, cause claim inquiries, and create audit risk, potentially resulting in denied or recouped payments.
examples: Encounter notes entered late leading to mismatch between billed service date and documentation → payer audit.
Delayed documentation prevents timely coding and claim submission within 12 months.

dependencies.strength_justification: Record retention requires first that records are created/updated; timely documentation is the upstream step before retention obligations are fulfilled.
dependencies.rationale: Retention policies specify minimum storage periods; those policies only apply when there is a record to store. Thus documentation creates the artifacts subject to retention.
dependencies.impact: Failure to document timely may produce gaps in retained records and increase legal/regulatory exposure during investigations or audits.
examples: Encounter notes not entered → gap exists in retained record for that care episode.
Delayed documentation results in late archival and potential compliance issues with retention schedules.

dependencies.strength_justification: These two rules specify different deadlines for updating medical records (24 hours vs 48 hours). They conflict when both claim applicability to the same encounter.
dependencies.rationale: Rules that set differing time-bound obligations for the same artifact introduce a contradiction that must be resolved by policy hierarchy (e.g., follow the stricter regulatory requirement or the accreditor standard).
dependencies.impact: Conflicting update timeframes create operational ambiguity, inconsistent compliance monitoring, and unreliable automated alerting.
examples: Clinician follows 48-hour rule while accreditation survey enforces 24-hour rule → survey citation.
EHR automation configured to 24-hour alerts while unit policy references 48 hours → mixed adherence and compliance reporting errors.

dependencies.strength_justification: Deficiency reporting is triggered by findings during CMS compliance reviews/surveys — reviews are the triggering event for the 30-day reporting obligation.
dependencies.rationale: CMS conducts reviews/audits; if deficiencies are identified during these reviews, facilities are obligated to report within 30 days. The review is therefore a prerequisite/trigger for the deficiency-reporting timeline.
dependencies.impact: If CMS reviews are not conducted or documented, deficiency timelines cannot be initiated properly, impeding regulatory remediation and risking penalties.
examples: CMS survey finds noncompliance; facility must report corrective action plans within 30 days.
No survey performed; no deficiency-reporting cycle is started, but regulatory obligations remain unmet.

dependencies.strength_justification: If a facility must pass an annual CMS survey, any deficiencies identified during that survey trigger the deficiency-reporting requirement; the survey is the event that generates the report.
dependencies.rationale: Passing/being surveyed annually creates the inspection context. When the survey identifies deficiencies, the facility must report them within the 30-day timeframe, so the survey -> report is a sequential flow.
dependencies.impact: If surveys are not completed or deficiencies not captured, timely reporting and remediation cannot be executed, increasing enforcement risk.
examples: Survey indicates medication storage noncompliance; facility files required deficiency report within 30 days.
Survey skipped or incomplete; deficiency reporting timeline cannot be initiated properly.

dependencies.strength_justification: Annual coverage disclosure and the requirement to communicate benefits at least once a year are mutually reinforcing member-communication obligations.
dependencies.rationale: Both rules address the obligation to inform members about coverage and benefit changes on an annual cadence; they complement each other to ensure members are informed.
dependencies.impact: Failure to implement annual coverage disclosures undermines the 'communicate at least once a year' obligation, increasing regulatory exposure and member complaints.
examples: Plan issues annual Summary of Benefits and Coverage and also sends member notices during open enrollment.
Plan performs one but not the other, resulting in incomplete member communications.

dependencies.strength_justification: Strong override: 24-hour requirement supersedes a looser 48-hour timeframe when both apply.
dependencies.rationale: BR_TREATS_RELATIONSHIP_012_001 requires documentation within 24 hours and BR_TREATS_REGULATORY_025_001 allows up to 48 hours. The 24-hour rule is the stricter operational expectation and will govern compliance assessments if both are present.
dependencies.impact: If documentation is completed between 24 and 48 hours, the organization may be compliant with the 48-hour rule but non-compliant with the 24-hour rule and subject to sanctions tied to the stricter standard.
examples: Documentation at 30 hours → violation of 24-hour rule but not 48-hour rule; accreditor may cite the stricter requirement if adopted locally.
Documentation at 12 hours → compliant with both rules (override satisfied).

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate conflict: two co-existing rules prescribe different timeframes (48 hours vs 24 hours) — this creates ambiguity that requires resolution by policy (e.g., adopt stricter standard or define scope).
dependencies.rationale: BR_TREATS_REGULATORY_006_001 specifies documentation within 48 hours; BR_TREATS_RELATIONSHIP_012_001 specifies within 24 hours. They cannot both be satisfied as equivalent obligations; the organization must determine which requirement governs (e.g., via higher-authority/regulatory hierarchy or local policy).
dependencies.impact: If the organization follows the 48-hour standard where the 24-hour standard is expected by an accreditor/regulator, it may receive citations; conversely, enforcing the 24-hour rule where only 48 hours is required is operationally stricter but acceptable.
examples: Local policy elects to adopt 24-hour requirement -> 48-hour rule becomes a lesser-bound alternative.
Accreditor enforces 24-hour rule but documentation policy allowed 48 hours -> accreditation finding issued.

dependencies.strength_justification: Moderate: meeting the 24-hour documentation requirement provides concrete evidence that the general rule to document each treatment has been satisfied.
dependencies.rationale: BR_TREATS_RELATIONSHIP_003_001 requires that treatments be documented. BR_TREATS_RELATIONSHIP_012_001 adds a 24-hour timeframe. Completion of the 24-hour documentation event provides the validation artifacts needed to show the general documentation requirement has been met.
dependencies.impact: If 24-hour documentation is not produced, auditors may not be able to validate that the general documentation requirement for each treatment was satisfied, leading to citations.
examples: Treatment note recorded within 24 hours → verifies that the treatment was documented as required.
No documentation within 24 hours → lack of evidence to validate the general documentation requirement.

dependencies.strength_justification: Plan requires medical necessity documentation to evaluate and grant authorizations; this materially affects ability to grant authorization.
dependencies.rationale: Health plan authorization processes typically require evidence of medical necessity before granting authorization. If the plan will not evaluate or grant authorizations without that documentation, then medical-necessity documentation is a prerequisite for obtaining an authorization.
dependencies.impact: If medical necessity documentation is not provided, authorization will be delayed or denied and the surgical procedure may not be approved for coverage.
examples: Surgeon submits an authorization request without operative indication notes; plan returns request as incomplete.
Pre-authorization denied because supporting imaging/report demonstrating necessity was not included.

dependencies.strength_justification: 040_002 imposes a timeframe on the authorization step described in 003_001; it's a timing refinement rather than a separate legal blocker.
dependencies.rationale: Rule 003_001 requires authorization; rule 040_002 specifies that health plans must provide authorization within 48 hours of receiving the request. The timeframe is a sequential/time-based constraint of the authorization process.
dependencies.impact: If plans do not meet the 48-hour requirement, surgeries may be postponed; however lack of the timeframe does not change the fundamental requirement that authorization exists before proceeding (except where emergency exceptions apply).
examples: Authorization request submitted Monday afternoon; insurer must respond within 48 hours per policy 040_002.
Provider follows up after 72 hours due to missed 48-hour response window, causing scheduling delays.

dependencies.strength_justification: Documented authorization must exist before scheduling/performing the procedure; procedure performance triggers post-procedure documentation obligations.
dependencies.rationale: BR_AUTHORIZES_RELATIONSHIP_011_010 requires that authorization be documented pre-procedure. BR_DOCUMENTS_RELATIONSHIP_027_001 requires immediate documentation of the surgical procedure after performance. The authorization documentation step precedes the procedural documentation step.
dependencies.impact: If authorization is not documented pre-procedure, the subsequent required procedure documentation does not protect reimbursement or compliance; the provider risks denial despite completing post-op notes.
examples: Pre-op chart contains insurer approval; post-op operative note entered immediately after procedure as required.
No pre-op authorization is documented; although operative notes are filed immediately, insurer denies coverage.

dependencies.strength_justification: Immediate post-procedure documentation is stricter than 'within 24 hours' — the stricter requirement overrides the more permissive timeframe where both apply.
dependencies.rationale: BR_DOCUMENTS_RELATIONSHIP_027_001 mandates documentation immediately following procedure. BR_DOCUMENTS_RELATIONSHIP_001_001 permits documentation within 24 hours. When both are relevant, the immediate requirement overrides the 24-hour allowance for timeliness of documentation.
dependencies.impact: Where 'immediate' applies, using a 24-hour window is insufficient and may be non-compliant; workflows must comply with the stricter immediate documentation obligation.
examples: Hospital policy states operative notes must be completed in the PACU before discharge (immediate) even though some regulatory texts allow 24 hours.
If accreditation standard requires immediate documentation, relying on the 24-hour rule will trigger non-compliance findings.

dependencies.strength_justification: Accurate, timely documentation is commonly required to validate claims prior to submission; claims submission depends on having the procedural documentation available.
dependencies.rationale: BR_DOCUMENTS_RELATIONSHIP_001_001 requires surgical procedures be documented within 24 hours. Claim submission rules rely on that documentation to support billing; without the documentation, claims are likely to be denied or down-coded.
dependencies.impact: If documentation is incomplete or missing at time of claim submission, claims may be denied, delayed, or audited.
examples: Insurance requests operative note to substantiate CPT code billed; missing note leads to denial.
Coder waits for finalized operative note before submitting claim to meet documentation-based coverage rules.

dependencies.strength_justification: Prior authorization is a legal/billing precondition for coverage for many elective/non-emergency procedures; absence is a direct cause of claim denial.
dependencies.rationale: BR_AUTHORIZES_RELATIONSHIP_003_001 requires plan authorization before performance of surgical procedures. If authorization is required for coverage, then claim submission and expected reimbursement depend on having had prior authorization.
dependencies.impact: Submitting claims for procedures performed without required authorization is likely to result in denials, appeals, and potential patient financial responsibility.
examples: Elective surgery performed without pre-auth; insurer denies the claim for lack of authorization.
Pre-auth is on file and claim paid without authorization-related denials.

dependencies.strength_justification: 003_001 states authorization for 'all surgical procedures' whereas 022_001 explicitly scopes to 'elective' procedures only — this is a direct scope conflict of moderate-to-high operational impact.
dependencies.rationale: A rule that mandates authorization for all procedures conflicts with a rule that restricts requirement to elective procedures. The conflict must be resolved by policy hierarchy (e.g., emergency exceptions) or clarification.
dependencies.impact: Unresolved, teams may misapply prior-auth requirements and either delay urgent care or incur financial risk through unapproved elective procedures.
examples: Clinician unsure if emergent surgery requires pre-auth; hospital policy needs to clarify exception for emergencies.
Billing denies a claim because a post-emergency authorization was not obtained, despite 022_001 suggesting only elective cases require pre-auth.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules govern documentation timing and completeness; together they form a cohesive documentation standard for compliance.
dependencies.rationale: Multiple documentation rules operate together to ensure medical records meet regulatory and internal policy expectations; they are complementary rather than independent obligations.
dependencies.impact: Failing one documentation requirement increases regulatory and legal risk; aligning both ensures stronger compliance posture.
examples: 24-hour operative note requirement (001_001) plus regulatory documentation expectations (025_001) create combined checklist for clinicians.
QA uses both rules to audit completeness and timeliness of post-op notes.

dependencies.strength_justification: 031_001 and 001_001 both set the same 24-hour documentation expectation; they reinforce each other and operate together to ensure timeliness.
dependencies.rationale: These documentation rules are overlapping and together provide redundancy and support for enforcement and auditing of post-op documentation timelines.
dependencies.impact: Using both rules in audits reduces chance of missing non-compliant instances and provides multiple citations for remediation.
examples: Clinical documentation improvement program references both rules when educating surgeons about operative note deadlines.
EMR builds an alert based on the 24-hour requirement enforced by both rules.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both require documentation immediately/shortly after completion; together they ensure comprehensive record keeping across settings.
dependencies.rationale: Complementary documentation rules ensure consistent expectations regardless of care setting and reduce gaps in the medical record.
dependencies.impact: Aligned documentation rules reduce regulatory exposure and support coding/billing activities.
examples: Post-op note entered in hospital EHR and replicated in ambulatory record per complementary requirements.
Multisite care teams follow both rules to ensure consistent documentation standards across facilities.

dependencies.strength_justification: Authorization must be both granted and documented; the existence of an authorization (003_001) validates the requirement that authorization documentation be present (039_010).
dependencies.rationale: 039_010 requires documentation for each authorization prior to the procedure. The authorization action is the source event that must be evidenced in the record.
dependencies.impact: If authorizations are not documented, claims and compliance reviews will fail even if an approval was verbally given.
examples: Insurer approval number must be retained in chart as required proof per 039_010.
Verbal approvals without documentation lead to audit findings.

dependencies.strength_justification: The two documentation rules are overlapping statements of the same obligation; treating them as complementary strengthens enforcement.
dependencies.rationale: Both require documentation within 24 hours for surgical procedures; using them together provides redundant controls and clarity.
dependencies.impact: Reinforces that documented operative notes are required within 24 hours across policy statements.
examples: Audit citations use both citation points when operative notes are late.
Clinical education references both rules to emphasize the 24-hour standard.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules apply to completion of documentation after surgical procedures across settings — they operate together for comprehensive coverage.
dependencies.rationale: Complementary documentation obligations ensure that no matter the setting, documentation timing and completeness expectations are applied consistently.
dependencies.impact: Consistency across documentation rules reduces the risk of incomplete records and supports billing and quality reporting.
examples: Inpatient and ambulatory surgical records both adhere to 24-hour documentation standards derived from these complementary rules.
QA uses combined criteria from both rules when running documentation completeness reports.

dependencies.strength_justification: Written informed consent is legally required before surgical procedures; without consent the procedure should not proceed and documentation will reflect lack of authorization.
dependencies.rationale: Surgical procedure documentation depends on valid, documented consent because the consent authorizes the procedure that the documentation records. Auditors and legal reviewers require proof consent existed prior to the procedure.
dependencies.impact: If consent is not obtained first, the procedure may be unauthorized with legal/CMS consequences; documentation alone cannot remediate lack of consent and may trigger investigations and penalties.
examples: Elective knee arthroscopy scheduled without a signed consent form—surgery performed and later flagged during audit.
Consent obtained and stored in chart before procedure—surgical note references consent and satisfies surveyors.

dependencies.strength_justification: Patient identity verification using two unique identifiers is a critical safety step before any procedure; performing the correct procedure on the correct patient is fundamental.
dependencies.rationale: Surgical documentation presumes the correct patient underwent the procedure; if identity verification is not performed first, the documentation may reflect care given to the wrong person and create severe safety/legal consequences.
dependencies.impact: Failure to verify identity can lead to wrong-patient surgery, sentinel events, regulatory action and malpractice exposure.
examples: Two-identifier check completed in pre-op holding; surgical note references verified identifiers.
Identifiers not confirmed—surgery cancelled when discrepancy found between patient's wristband and chart.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules require timely updating of the medical record after treatment; surgical documentation is a domain-specific application of the general record-update requirement.
dependencies.rationale: Surgical notes are a subset of the patient record updates required within 24 hours of treatment; both work together to ensure completeness and timeliness of the medical record.
dependencies.impact: If surgical documentation is not completed timely, the facility may fail the general 24-hour update requirement and be cited during audits.
examples: Surgical note filed within 24 hours → supports the medical record update requirement.
Surgery documented late → general record-update rule cited in compliance review.

dependencies.strength_justification: The 48-hour 'complete medical record' requirement and the 24-hour surgical documentation timeframe overlap and can appear inconsistent about the expected completion timing for related artifacts.
dependencies.rationale: A site could interpret 'complete record within 48 hours' as the controlling requirement while surgical rules require 24-hour documentation; overlapping timeframes create ambiguity for staff and auditors.
dependencies.impact: Confusion over which timeframe controls may lead to inconsistent documentation practices and audit findings; policy harmonization is needed to avoid contradictory expectations.
examples: Facility policy cites 48-hour completion for admission records while surgical services apply a 24-hour requirement—staff uncertain which to follow.
Surveyor cites 24-hour surgical documentation while other departments reference 48-hour requirement—leads to mixed compliance responses.

dependencies.strength_justification: This rule explicitly requires documented consent be present in the medical record for surgical procedures—this validates that consent exists and is captured in documentation.
dependencies.rationale: Surgical documentation should reference and include the consent form; documentation completion includes validation that consent is present as required by law and accreditation standards.
dependencies.impact: If consent is not documented in the record, the surgical documentation is incomplete and may trigger legal/regulatory action and audit findings.
examples: Operative note references attached consent form and signatory details → validation of compliance.
Operative note lacks attached consent → surveyor cites missing consent documentation.

dependencies.strength_justification: Completion of the medical record within set timeframe (48 hours) is a common accreditation performance indicator; it's a strong dependency for compliance.
dependencies.rationale: Joint Commission expects records to be completed in a timely manner; the 48-hour completion requirement supports the facility's ability to demonstrate adherence to documentation standards.
dependencies.impact: If records are not completed within the 48-hour requirement, the organization may be cited during accreditation surveys with related consequences.
examples: Post-admission notes and operative reports not finalized within 48 hours -> deficiency observed.
Policies and monitoring show >10% of charts incomplete at 48 hours -> triggers corrective action.

dependencies.strength_justification: A 30-day update window supports compliance but is a less strict timeframe; still relevant to accreditation but lower immediacy than 24/48-hour rules.
dependencies.rationale: Updating medical records within 30 days is a stated requirement; while it supports accreditation, many accreditation checks focus on timelier documentation, so this dependency is moderate.
dependencies.impact: If records are not updated within 30 days, it can lead to audit findings and compliance issues, though less immediate than shorter timeframe failures.
examples: Late entry of procedure note beyond 30 days found on audit -> noncompliance for record completeness.
Billing and coding reconciliations fail due to missing 30-day updates, triggering financial and compliance reviews.

dependencies.strength_justification: Documentation of consent is a strong validation requirement for surgical documentation; surgical notes must include proof of informed consent to be compliant.
dependencies.rationale: BR_PATIENT_SAFETY_011_001 requires that informed consent be obtained and documented in the patient's record prior to surgical procedures. BR_DOCUMENTS_RELATIONSHIP_001_001 requires that surgical procedures be documented within 24 hours. The surgical documentation obligation implicitly depends on inclusion/validation of consent in the record.
dependencies.impact: If consent is not documented (source not satisfied), surgical documentation will be incomplete and non-compliant; audits could find missing consent and trigger legal/CMS consequences.
examples: A surgical note is completed within 24 hours but lacks the signed informed consent form — audit deficiency and claim denial risk.
Consent recorded as verbal in chart without signature, failing the documented-consent requirement during survey.

dependencies.strength_justification: Timely surgical-procedure note entry (24 hours) is a necessary step toward completing the overall medical record within the admission timeframe (48 hours).
dependencies.rationale: BR_DOCUMENTS_RELATIONSHIP_001_001 requires documentation of surgical procedures within 24 hours; BR_PATIENT_DOCUMENTATION_008_001 requires that the patient's medical record be completed within 48 hours of admission. The procedure-level documentation is a sequential step feeding into completion of the full record.
dependencies.impact: If procedure notes are not entered within 24 hours, completing the full record within 48 hours becomes unlikely, increasing audit deficiency risk.
examples: Surgeon completes operative note within 12 hours, enabling 48-hour completion of the full admission record.
Delay in operative note entry causes chart completion to miss the 48-hour requirement.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules state the 12-month claim submission timeline and together form a single billing compliance requirement for claims timeliness.
dependencies.rationale: Provider-side and plan-side claim submission timeframes must align; they reinforce the single operational deadline of 12 months.
dependencies.impact: Failure to submit within 12 months typically results in denial and lost reimbursement.
examples: Provider submits surgical claim within 12 months to meet both internal and payer rules.
Late claim submitted after 12 months is denied per both rules.

dependencies.strength_justification: Authorization-documentation must occur before the procedure; the surgical procedure report is generated immediately after the procedure — proper ordering is required for compliance and billing.
dependencies.rationale: The sequence is: authorization documented (pre-procedure) → procedure performed → surgical report completed (immediate/post-procedure). If the pre-procedure authorization step is missing, the downstream documentation and billing chain is at risk.
dependencies.impact: If authorization is not documented beforehand, the subsequent surgical documentation may not protect against payment denial; scheduling and billing may be invalidated.
examples: Pre-op checklist shows prior authorization completed; post-op report is then completed immediately per the workflow.
If the authorization box is unchecked pre-op, billing denies the claim despite completed surgery reports.

dependencies.strength_justification: Rules that restate the same requirement (document within 24 hours) are complementary controls.
dependencies.rationale: This rule set collectively enforces the single standard; they should be aligned operationally and in the EMR.
dependencies.impact: Non-alignment across complementary documentation rules can create procedural gaps and audit weaknesses.
examples: An OR nurse verifies that the operative note exists to satisfy all applicable documentation statements.
An audit cross-references these complementary rules when checking compliance.

dependencies.strength_justification: Documentation of authorization prior to procedure is a condition that materially affects reimbursement decisions.
dependencies.rationale: If authorization is not properly documented prior to the procedure, payers may deny payment even if the claim is timely; documented prior auth supports claim adjudication.
dependencies.impact: Lack of documented pre-procedure authorization increases risk of claim denial and financial exposure.
examples: A claim is denied because the audit shows no prior-authorization document was present before the surgery.
Billing holds are triggered pending validation of authorization documentation.

dependencies.strength_justification: Documented authorization before the procedure is a core precondition for claims being considered payable by many plans; absence is a primary legal/coverage blocker.
dependencies.rationale: Claim adjudication depends on evidence the procedure was authorized per plan policy. If authorization was not documented prior to the procedure, the claim is likely to be denied regardless of timely submission.
dependencies.impact: If authorization is not documented, claims may be denied even if submitted within the 12-month timeframe; this creates financial liability for provider/patient and requires appeals or retro-authorizations.
examples: Authorization documented in the chart prior to surgery → claim submitted within 12 months and paid.
No documented authorization prior to surgery → claim denied; provider must pursue appeal or patient becomes liable.

dependencies.strength_justification: Documented authorization prior to procedure validates the provider's right to seek reimbursement; this validation function is strong in claims adjudication.
dependencies.rationale: Claims processors and auditors look for evidence of prior authorization in the record; documentation of authorization directly supports claim acceptance independent of submission timing.
dependencies.impact: Without the documented authorization artifact, claims are at high risk of denial and may require appeals or retroactive authorization processes.
examples: Authorization form present in chart → claim passes initial validation checks.
No authorization documentation → automated claim edit triggers denial.

PATIENT.definition: An individual receiving medical care or treatment.
examples: John Doe
Jane Smith
business_rules: Patient consent must be documented prior to any elective procedure.
All patient information must be kept confidential according to HIPAA regulations.

ATTENDING_PHYSICIAN.definition: A licensed medical professional responsible for the treatment of a patient.
examples: Dr. Alice Johnson
Dr. Bob Brown
business_rules: An attending physician must be present during surgical procedures.
The physician must document all patient interactions in the medical record.

SURGICAL_PROCEDURE.definition: A medical operation performed to treat a disease or injury.
examples: Appendectomy
Knee Replacement
business_rules: All surgical procedures require pre-authorization from the health plan.
Documentation of surgical procedures must be completed within 24 hours post-operation.

MEDICAL_RECORD.definition: A comprehensive document that contains the patient's medical history and treatments.
examples: Record ID 12345 for John Doe
Record ID 67890 for Jane Smith
business_rules: Medical records must be maintained for a minimum of seven years.
Patients have the right to access their medical records upon request.

HEALTH_PLAN.definition: An organization that provides health insurance coverage to members.
examples: Health Plan A
Health Plan B
business_rules: Health plans must provide a summary of benefits to all subscribers.
Claims must be submitted within 90 days of service to ensure reimbursement.

INSURANCE_CLAIM.definition: A request made to a health insurance company for payment for services received.
examples: Claim ID 1234 for John Doe
Claim ID 5678 for Jane Smith
business_rules: Claims must be submitted electronically to expedite processing.
Claim denials must be communicated to the provider within 30 days.

CMS_REGIONAL_OFFICE.definition: A local office of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services overseeing compliance and regulatory matters.
examples: CMS Office in New York
CMS Office in California
business_rules: CMS regional offices must conduct annual reviews of participating facilities.
All facilities must report compliance data to their CMS regional office.

JOINT_COMMISSION_STANDARD.definition: Standards set by The Joint Commission to ensure quality and safety in healthcare organizations.
examples: Standard 1: Patient Safety
Standard 2: Infection Control
business_rules: Healthcare organizations must comply with all applicable Joint Commission standards.
Non-compliance with standards may result in accreditation loss.

TREATS.definition: An attending physician provides medical treatment to a patient.
examples: Dr. Alice Johnson treats multiple patients in her practice.
Dr. Bob Brown treats a patient with a chronic condition.
business_rules: Each patient must have an assigned attending physician.
Physicians must document all treatments provided to patients.

AUTHORIZES.definition: A health plan grants approval for a surgical procedure to be performed.
examples: Health Plan A authorizes an appendectomy for Patient John Doe.
Health Plan B authorizes a knee replacement for Patient Jane Smith.
business_rules: Surgical procedures require prior authorization from the health plan.
Unauthorized procedures may not be reimbursed.

DOCUMENTS.definition: A surgical procedure is recorded in the patient's medical record.
examples: The appendectomy performed on John Doe is documented in his medical record.
The knee replacement for Jane Smith is recorded in her medical record.
business_rules: All surgical procedures must be documented in the patient's medical record within 24 hours.
Medical records must accurately reflect the procedures performed.

SUBMITS.definition: An acute care hospital submits claims to health plans for services rendered.
examples: The hospital submits multiple claims for surgeries performed in a month.
Claims are submitted to the health plan for reimbursement.
business_rules: Claims must be submitted within 90 days of service.
Hospitals must maintain records of all submitted claims.

REGULATES.definition: The CMS regional office oversees compliance and regulatory matters for the hospital.
examples: The CMS regional office conducts annual reviews of the hospital.
The hospital submits compliance data to the CMS regional office.
business_rules: Hospitals must comply with all regulations set forth by the CMS.
Failure to comply may result in penalties or loss of funding.
